revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: '春日神社の獅子舞は、いなべ市の無形文化財で300年以上の歴史があるといわれています。（三重県いなべ市藤原町下野尻...'
revision.text: 春日神社の獅子舞は、いなべ市の無形文化財で300年以上の歴史があるといわれています。（三重県いなべ市藤原町下野尻字炭焼き951番地）
{{sdnd}} 春日神社の獅子舞は、いなべ市の無形文化財で300年以上の歴史があるといわれています。（三重県いなべ市藤原町下野尻字炭焼き951番地）

{{sdnd}} 春日神社の獅子舞は、いなべ市の無形文化財で300年以上の歴史があるといわれています。現在使われている獅子頭は嘉永2年（1849）に作られたもので、およそ160年間大切に使われてきた貴重なものです。獅子舞は春と秋を表す「狂乱牡丹の舞」と「菊の舞」の2つの舞があり、豊作と地域の安全を祈願し行われています。これらの舞は、四方から襲ってくる災厄を追い払いながら毛繕いをしたり眠ったりと、動物の自然の仕草を取り入れた素朴な舞です。囃子（はやし）は、絣（かすり）の着物に花笠をつけたかわいらしい子どもたちが、大人の笛に合わせて太鼓や鉦などの楽器を奏でます。天狗は獅子に寄り添い、舞の案内や手助けをします。勇壮かつ優雅な獅子舞は、いつしか幽玄の世界へと引き込まれるような心和む秋の行事です。いなべ市指定無形民族文化財（三重県いなべ市藤原町下野尻字炭焼き951番地）

{{Sakujo/本体|2010年1月8日|春日神社（いなべ市）}}
<!-- 削除についての議論が終了するまで、上記部分は削除しないでください。 -->
{{Copyrights}}

春日神社の獅子舞は、いなべ市の無形文化財で300年以上の歴史があるといわれています。現在使われている獅子頭は嘉永2年（1849）に作られたもので、およそ160年間大切に使われてきた貴重なものです。獅子舞は春と秋を表す「狂乱牡丹の舞」と「菊の舞」の2つの舞があり、豊作と地域の安全を祈願し行われています。これらの舞は、四方から襲ってくる災厄を追い払いながら毛繕いをしたり眠ったりと、動物の自然の仕草を取り入れた素朴な舞です。囃子（はやし）は、絣（かすり）の着物に花笠をつけたかわいらしい子どもたちが、大人の笛に合わせて太鼓や鉦などの楽器を奏でます。天狗は獅子に寄り添い、舞の案内や手助けをします。勇壮かつ優雅な獅子舞は、いつしか幽玄の世界へと引き込まれるような心和む秋の行事です。いなべ市指定無形民族文化財（三重県いなべ市藤原町下野尻字炭焼き951番地）

revision.comment: 「[[春日神社（いなべ市）]]」を「[[春日神社 (いなべ市)]]」へ移動: とりあえず移動。[[Wikipedia:記事名の付け方#全角と半角の使い分け]]
revision.text: 春日神社の獅子舞は、いなべ市の無形文化財で300年以上の歴史があるといわれています。現在使われている獅子頭は嘉永2年（1849）に作られたもので、およそ160年間大切に使われてきた貴重なものです。獅子舞は春と秋を表す「狂乱牡丹の舞」と「菊の舞」の2つの舞があり、豊作と地域の安全を祈願し行われています。これらの舞は、四方から襲ってくる災厄を追い払いながら毛繕いをしたり眠ったりと、動物の自然の仕草を取り入れた素朴な舞です。囃子（はやし）は、絣（かすり）の着物に花笠をつけたかわいらしい子どもたちが、大人の笛に合わせて太鼓や鉦などの楽器を奏でます。天狗は獅子に寄り添い、舞の案内や手助けをします。勇壮かつ優雅な獅子舞は、いつしか幽玄の世界へと引き込まれるような心和む秋の行事です。いなべ市指定無形民族文化財（三重県いなべ市藤原町下野尻字炭焼き951番地）

{{神社 |名称 = 春日神社 |画像 = |所在地 = 三重県いなべ市藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |祭神 = 少名彦命 他10柱 |社格 = 旧村社 |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = |別名 = |札所 = |例祭 = 10月8日 |神事 = 奉納獅子舞（10月9・10日） }}
'''春日神社'''（かすがじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[いなべ市]][[藤原町 (三重県)|藤原町]]に鎮座する[[神社]]。[[旧社格]]は[[村社]]。
== 祭神 == [[少名彦命]]、[[大山祇神]]、[[火産霊神]]、[[八衢比古神]]、[[八衢比売神]]、[[岐の神|久那戸神（くなどのかみ）]]、[[磐長姫命]]、[[武甕槌神]]、[[天児屋根命]]、[[経津主命]]、[[罔象女神]]の11柱を祀る。
祭神中、少名彦命は「石に御霊を留座する神」であるという<ref>応永12年8月10日付「石神社石祭投石の事」（『三重県神社誌』所引）。</ref>。
== 由緒 == [[勧請]]年月等不明。古くは「伊原宮」と称した<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』。</ref>。『員弁郡郷土資料』は当神社を「石神社」として[[式内社]]である「[[伊勢国]][[員弁郡]]石神社」に比定して祭神を少名彦命とし、境内に夜ごとに光りを発する霊石が存し、これを敬った[[春澄善縄]]が社を建立して「石神社」と称したとの伝えを記す<ref>員弁郡役所編、『員弁郡郷土資料』、[[大正]]4年（1915年）刊。</ref>。『伊勢輯雑記』に、白瀬七郷の[[惣社]]として往時は大社の結構であったと記し、『員弁雑誌』は現下野尻、西野尻両町の[[産土神]]であるとし、これも「往古は壮大なる社なりし」と記す<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』は員弁郡の人である松宮祐重（[[天明]]7年（1787年） - [[弘化]]2年（1845年））の著。『員弁雑誌』は岡田静堂が明治初年に、『伊勢輯雑記』中から員弁郡の部を訂正増補して著録しもの。</ref>。[[文政]]7年（[[1824年]]）の「村明細帳」には「春日大明神」と記され、[[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）の「村明細帳」には「石原神社」とされており<ref>2つの「明細帳」はともに[[徳川林政史研究所]]所蔵。明治の「明細帳」に見える「石原」は「伊原」の誤記である可能性がある（『三重県の地名』）。</ref>、明治45年に村内の八天宮、水神、山神、塞神という小祠を[[合祀]]した<ref>文政明細帳は村内小祠として「愛宕、山神二」を挙げ、明治明細帳は「山神二、水神、走井神」を挙げる。また、『員弁雑誌』は八天宮を「八天狗宮」と、愛宕は「祠なし」と紹介する。</ref>。
== 祭祀 == 明治以前は8月14日が祭日であった（『員弁雑誌』）。『員弁郡郷土資料』によれば、祭礼に際して[[氏子]]は白石を捧げた後に「投石の神事」があったが、[[応永|応永年中]]（14世紀末から15世紀前葉）にそれに事寄せて[[石合戦]]が行われたために禁止されたと伝える<ref>前掲注「石祭投石の事」に、石を社前に捧げるのは「石に留座する」祭神との関係で理解できるが、投石の謂われはなく、危険なので、神役人の他は禁止する旨が記されている。</ref>。
=== 獅子舞 === 10月9日、10日の両日行われ、[[平成]]22年（[[2010年]]）時点で300年以上の歴史があるといわれる。現在使われている獅子頭は[[嘉永]]2年（[[1849年]]）に作られた貴重なもの。[[獅子舞]]は[[獅子]]と[[天狗]]役で演じられ、獅子は春と秋を表す「狂乱牡丹の舞」と「菊の舞」の2つの舞を、天狗は獅子に寄り添って舞の案内や手助けをする。獅子の舞は四方から襲ってくる災厄を追い払いながら毛繕いをしたり眠ったりと、動物の自然の仕草を取り入れた素朴な舞で、豊作と地域の安全を祈願する。また[[囃子]]（はやし）も奏されるが、これは[[絣]]の着物に花笠をつけた子どもたちが、大人の笛に合わせて[[太鼓]]や[[鉦]]などの楽器を奏でるものである。「下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞」として[[昭和]]58年（[[1983年]]）5月にいなべ市指定[[無形民俗文化財]]に登録された。
==文化財== *下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *『三重県神社誌』、三重県神社庁、平成5年 *『角川日本地名大辞典 三重県』（角川日本地名大辞典第24巻）、[[角川書店]]、昭和58年 *『三重県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系24巻）、[[平凡社]]、昭和58年
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm 三重県の無形民俗文化財一覧]（三重県教育委員会）

祭神中、少名彦命は「石に御霊を留座する神」であるという<ref>応永12年8月10日付「石神社石祭投石の事」（『三重県神社誌』所引）。</ref>。

=== 獅子舞 === 10月9日、10日の両日行われ、[[平成]]22年（[[2010年]]）時点で300年以上の歴史があるといわれる。現在使われている獅子頭は[[嘉永]]2年（[[1849年]]）に作られた貴重なもの。[[獅子舞]]は[[獅子]]と[[天狗]]役で演じられ、獅子は春と秋を表す「狂乱牡丹の舞」と「菊の舞」の2つの舞を、天狗は獅子に寄り添って舞の案内や手助けをする。獅子の舞は四方から襲ってくる災厄を追い払いながら毛繕いをしたり眠ったりと、動物の自然の仕草を取り入れた素朴な舞で、豊作と地域の安全を祈願する。また[[囃子]]（はやし）も奏されるが、これは[[絣]]の着物に花笠をつけた子どもたちが、大人の笛に合わせて[[太鼓]]や[[鉦]]などの楽器を奏でるものである。「下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞」として[[昭和]]58年（[[1983年]]）5月にいなべ市指定[[無形民俗文化財]]に登録された。

== 由緒 == [[勧請]]年月等不明。古くは「伊原宮」と称した<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』。</ref>。『員弁郡郷土資料』は当神社を「石神社」として[[式内社]]である「[[伊勢国]][[員弁郡]]石神社」に比定して祭神を少名彦命とし、境内に夜ごとに光を発する霊石が存し、これを敬った[[春澄善縄]]が社を建立して「石神社」と称したとの伝えを記す<ref>員弁郡役所編、『員弁郡郷土資料』、[[大正]]4年（1915年）刊。</ref>。『伊勢輯雑記』に、白瀬七郷の[[惣社]]として往時は大社の結構であったと記し、『員弁雑誌』は現下野尻、西野尻両町の[[産土神]]であるとし、これも「往古は壮大なる社なりし」と記す<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』は員弁郡の人である松宮祐重（[[天明]]7年（1787年） - [[弘化]]2年（1845年））の著。『員弁雑誌』は岡田静堂が明治初年に、『伊勢輯雑記』中から員弁郡の部を訂正増補して著録しもの。</ref>。[[文政]]7年（[[1824年]]）の「村明細帳」には「春日大明神」と記され、[[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）の「村明細帳」には「石原神社」とされており<ref>2つの「明細帳」はともに[[徳川林政史研究所]]所蔵。明治の「明細帳」に見える「石原」は「伊原」の誤記である可能性がある（『三重県の地名』）。</ref>、明治45年に村内の八天宮、水神、山神、塞神という小祠を[[合祀]]した<ref>文政明細帳は村内小祠として「愛宕、山神二」を挙げ、明治明細帳は「山神二、水神、走井神」を挙げる。また、『員弁雑誌』は八天宮を「八天狗宮」と、愛宕は「祠なし」と紹介する。</ref>。
== 祭祀 == 明治以前は8月14日が祭日であった（『員弁雑誌』）。『員弁郡郷土資料』によれば、祭礼に際して[[氏子]]は白石を捧げた後に「投石の神事」があったが、[[応永|応永年中]]（14世紀末から15世紀前葉）にそれに事寄せて[[石合戦]]が行われたために禁止されたと伝える<ref>前掲注「石祭投石の事」に、石を社前に捧げるのは「石に留座する」祭神との関係で理解できるが、投石の謂われはなく、危険なので、神役人の他は禁止する旨が記されている。</ref>。
=== 獅子舞 === 10月9日、10日の両日行われ、[[平成]]22年（[[2010年]]）時点で300年以上の歴史があるといわれる。現在使われている獅子頭は[[嘉永]]2年（[[1849年]]）に作られた貴重なもの。[[獅子舞]]は[[獅子]]と[[天狗]]役で演じられ、獅子は春と秋を表す「狂乱牡丹の舞」と「菊の舞」の2つの舞を、天狗は獅子に寄り添って舞の案内や手助けをする。獅子の舞は四方から襲ってくる災厄を追い払いながら[[グルーミング|毛繕い]]をしたり眠ったりと、動物の自然の仕草を取り入れた素朴な舞で、豊作と地域の安全を祈願する。また[[囃子]]（はやし）も奏されるが、これは[[絣]]の着物に花笠をつけた子どもたちが、大人の笛に合わせて[[太鼓]]や[[鉦]]などの楽器を奏でるものである。「下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞」として[[昭和]]58年（[[1983年]]）5月にいなべ市指定[[無形民俗文化財]]に登録された。
==文化財== *下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞

{{神社 |名称 = 春日神社 |画像 = |所在地 = 三重県いなべ市藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |祭神 = 少名彦命 他10柱 |社格 = 旧村社 |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = |別名 = |札所 = |例祭 = 10月8日 |神事 = 奉納獅子舞（10月9・10日） }}
'''春日神社'''（かすがじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[いなべ市]][[藤原町 (三重県)|藤原町]]に鎮座する[[神社]]。[[旧社格]]は[[村社]]。
== 祭神 == [[少名彦命]]、[[大山祇神]]、[[火産霊神]]、[[八衢比古神]]、[[八衢比売神]]、[[岐の神|久那戸神（くなどのかみ）]]、[[磐長姫命]]、[[武甕槌神]]、[[天児屋根命]]、[[経津主命]]、[[罔象女神]]の11柱を祀る。
祭神中、少名彦命は「石に御霊を留座する神」であるという<ref>応永12年8月10日付「石神社石祭投石の事」（『三重県神社誌』所引）。</ref>。
== 由緒 == [[勧請]]年月等不明。古くは「伊原宮」と称した<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』。</ref>。『員弁郡郷土資料』は当神社を「石神社」として[[式内社]]である「[[伊勢国]][[員弁郡]]石神社」に比定して祭神を少名彦命とし、境内に夜ごとに光を発する霊石が存し、これを敬った[[春澄善縄]]が社を建立して「石神社」と称したとの伝えを記す<ref>員弁郡役所編、『員弁郡郷土資料』、[[大正]]4年（1915年）刊。</ref>。『伊勢輯雑記』に、白瀬七郷の[[惣社]]として往時は大社の結構であったと記し、『員弁雑誌』は現下野尻、西野尻両町の[[産土神]]であるとし、これも「往古は壮大なる社なりし」と記す<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』は員弁郡の人である松宮祐重（[[天明]]7年（1787年） - [[弘化]]2年（1845年））の著。『員弁雑誌』は岡田静堂が明治初年に、『伊勢輯雑記』中から員弁郡の部を訂正増補して著録しもの。</ref>。[[文政]]7年（[[1824年]]）の「村明細帳」には「春日大明神」と記され、[[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）の「村明細帳」には「石原神社」とされており<ref>2つの「明細帳」はともに[[徳川林政史研究所]]所蔵。明治の「明細帳」に見える「石原」は「伊原」の誤記である可能性がある（『三重県の地名』）。</ref>、明治45年に村内の八天宮、水神、山神、塞神という小祠を[[合祀]]した<ref>文政明細帳は村内小祠として「愛宕、山神二」を挙げ、明治明細帳は「山神二、水神、走井神」を挙げる。また、『員弁雑誌』は八天宮を「八天狗宮」と、愛宕は「祠なし」と紹介する。</ref>。
== 祭祀 == 明治以前は8月14日が祭日であった（『員弁雑誌』）。『員弁郡郷土資料』によれば、祭礼に際して[[氏子]]は白石を捧げた後に「投石の神事」があったが、[[応永|応永年中]]（14世紀末から15世紀前葉）にそれに事寄せて[[石合戦]]が行われたために禁止されたと伝える<ref>前掲注「石祭投石の事」に、石を社前に捧げるのは「石に留座する」祭神との関係で理解できるが、投石の謂われはなく、危険なので、神役人の他は禁止する旨が記されている。</ref>。
=== 獅子舞 === 10月9日、10日の両日行われ、[[平成]]22年（[[2010年]]）時点で300年以上の歴史があるといわれる。現在使われている獅子頭は[[嘉永]]2年（[[1849年]]）に作られた貴重なもの。[[獅子舞]]は[[獅子]]と[[天狗]]役で演じられ、獅子は春と秋を表す「狂乱牡丹の舞」と「菊の舞」の2つの舞を、天狗は獅子に寄り添って舞の案内や手助けをする。獅子の舞は四方から襲ってくる災厄を追い払いながら[[グルーミング|毛繕い]]をしたり眠ったりと、動物の自然の仕草を取り入れた素朴な舞で、豊作と地域の安全を祈願する。また[[囃子]]（はやし）も奏されるが、これは[[絣]]の着物に花笠をつけた子どもたちが、大人の笛に合わせて[[太鼓]]や[[鉦]]などの楽器を奏でるものである。「下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞」として[[昭和]]58年（[[1983年]]）5月にいなべ市指定[[無形民俗文化財]]に登録された。

== 由緒 == [[勧請]]年月等不明。古くは「伊原宮」と称した<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』。</ref>。『員弁郡郷土資料』は当神社を「石神社」として[[式内社]]である「[[伊勢国]][[員弁郡]]石神社」に比定して祭神を少名彦命とし、境内に夜ごとに光を発する霊石が存し、これを敬った[[春澄善縄]]が社を建立して「石神社」と称したとの伝えを記す<ref>員弁郡役所編、『員弁郡郷土資料』、[[大正]]4年（1915年）刊。</ref>。『伊勢輯雑記』に、白瀬七郷の[[惣社]]として往時は大社の結構であったと記し、『員弁雑誌』は現下野尻、西野尻両町の[[産土神]]であるとし、これも「往古は壮大なる社なりし」と記す<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』は員弁郡の人である松宮祐重（[[天明]]7年（1787年） - [[弘化]]2年（1845年））の著。『員弁雑誌』は岡田静堂が明治初年に、『伊勢輯雑記』中から員弁郡の部を訂正増補して著録しもの。</ref>。[[文政]]7年（[[1824年]]）の「村明細帳」には「春日大明神」と記され、[[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）の「村明細帳」には「石原神社」とされており<ref>2つの「明細帳」はともに[[徳川林政史研究所]]所蔵。明治の「明細帳」に見える「石原」は「伊原」の誤記である可能性がある（『三重県の地名』）。</ref>、明治45年に村内の八天宮、水神、山神、塞神という小祠を[[合祀]]した<ref>文政明細帳は村内小祠として「愛宕、山神二」を挙げ、明治明細帳は「山神二、水神、走井神」を挙げる。また、『員弁雑誌』は八天宮を「八天狗宮」と、愛宕は「祠なし」と紹介する。</ref>。
== 祭祀 == 明治以前は8月14日が祭日であった（『員弁雑誌』）。『員弁郡郷土資料』によれば、祭礼に際して[[氏子]]は白石を捧げた後に「投石の神事」があったが、[[応永|応永年中]]（14世紀末から15世紀前葉）にそれに事寄せて[[石合戦]]が行われたために禁止されたと伝える<ref>前掲注「石祭投石の事」に、石を社前に捧げるのは「石に留座する」祭神との関係で理解できるが、投石の謂われはなく、危険なので、神役人の他は禁止する旨が記されている。</ref>。
=== 獅子舞 === 10月9日、10日の両日行われ、[[平成]]22年（[[2010年]]）時点で300年以上の歴史があるといわれる。現在使われている獅子頭は[[嘉永]]2年（[[1849年]]）に作られた貴重なもの。[[獅子舞]]は[[獅子]]と[[天狗]]役で演じられ、獅子は春と秋を表す「狂乱牡丹の舞」と「菊の舞」の2つの舞を、天狗は獅子に寄り添って舞の案内や手助けをする。獅子の舞は四方から襲ってくる災厄を追い払いながら[[グルーミング|毛繕い]]をしたり眠ったりと、動物の自然の仕草を取り入れた素朴な舞で、豊作と地域の安全を祈願する。また[[囃子]]（はやし）も奏されるが、これは[[絣]]の着物に花笠をつけた子どもたちが、大人の笛に合わせて[[太鼓]]や[[鉦]]などの楽器を奏でるものである。「下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞」として[[昭和]]58年（[[1983年]]）5月にいなべ市指定[[無形民俗文化財]]に登録された。
==文化財== *下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞

{{神社 |名称 = 春日神社 |画像={{画像募集中|cat=いなべ市}} |所在地 = 三重県いなべ市藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |祭神 = 少名彦命 他10柱 |社格 = 旧村社 |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = |別名 = |札所 = |例祭 = 10月8日 |神事 = 奉納獅子舞（10月9・10日） }}
'''春日神社'''（かすがじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[いなべ市]][[藤原町 (三重県)|藤原町]]に鎮座する[[神社]]。[[旧社格]]は[[村社]]。
== 祭神 == [[少名彦命]]、[[大山祇神]]、[[火産霊神]]、[[八衢比古神]]、[[八衢比売神]]、[[岐の神|久那戸神（くなどのかみ）]]、[[磐長姫命]]、[[武甕槌神]]、[[天児屋根命]]、[[経津主命]]、[[罔象女神]]の11柱を祀る。
祭神中、少名彦命は「石に御霊を留座する神」であるという<ref>応永12年8月10日付「石神社石祭投石の事」（『三重県神社誌』所引）。</ref>。
== 由緒 == [[勧請]]年月等不明。古くは「伊原宮」と称した<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』。</ref>。『員弁郡郷土資料』は当神社を「石神社」として[[式内社]]である「[[伊勢国]][[員弁郡]]石神社」に比定して祭神を少名彦命とし、境内に夜ごとに光を発する霊石が存し、これを敬った[[春澄善縄]]が社を建立して「石神社」と称したとの伝えを記す<ref>員弁郡役所編、『員弁郡郷土資料』、[[大正]]4年（1915年）刊。</ref>。『伊勢輯雑記』に、白瀬七郷の[[惣社]]として往時は大社の結構であったと記し、『員弁雑誌』は現下野尻、西野尻両町の[[産土神]]であるとし、これも「往古は壮大なる社なりし」と記す<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』は員弁郡の人である松宮祐重（[[天明]]7年（1787年） - [[弘化]]2年（1845年））の著。『員弁雑誌』は岡田静堂が明治初年に、『伊勢輯雑記』中から員弁郡の部を訂正増補して著録しもの。</ref>。[[文政]]7年（[[1824年]]）の「村明細帳」には「春日大明神」と記され、[[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）の「村明細帳」には「石原神社」とされており<ref>2つの「明細帳」はともに[[徳川林政史研究所]]所蔵。明治の「明細帳」に見える「石原」は「伊原」の誤記である可能性がある（『三重県の地名』）。</ref>、明治45年に村内の八天宮、水神、山神、塞神という小祠を[[合祀]]した<ref>文政明細帳は村内小祠として「愛宕、山神二」を挙げ、明治明細帳は「山神二、水神、走井神」を挙げる。また、『員弁雑誌』は八天宮を「八天狗宮」と、愛宕は「祠なし」と紹介する。</ref>。
== 祭祀 == 明治以前は8月14日が祭日であった（『員弁雑誌』）。『員弁郡郷土資料』によれば、祭礼に際して[[氏子]]は白石を捧げた後に「投石の神事」があったが、[[応永|応永年中]]（14世紀末から15世紀前葉）にそれに事寄せて[[石合戦]]が行われたために禁止されたと伝える<ref>前掲注「石祭投石の事」に、石を社前に捧げるのは「石に留座する」祭神との関係で理解できるが、投石の謂われはなく、危険なので、神役人の他は禁止する旨が記されている。</ref>。
=== 獅子舞 === 10月9日、10日の両日行われ、[[平成]]22年（[[2010年]]）時点で300年以上の歴史があるといわれる。現在使われている獅子頭は[[嘉永]]2年（[[1849年]]）に作られた貴重なもの。[[獅子舞]]は[[獅子]]と[[天狗]]役で演じられ、獅子は春と秋を表す「狂乱牡丹の舞」と「菊の舞」の2つの舞を、天狗は獅子に寄り添って舞の案内や手助けをする。獅子の舞は四方から襲ってくる災厄を追い払いながら[[グルーミング|毛繕い]]をしたり眠ったりと、動物の自然の仕草を取り入れた素朴な舞で、豊作と地域の安全を祈願する。また[[囃子]]（はやし）も奏されるが、これは[[絣]]の着物に花笠をつけた子どもたちが、大人の笛に合わせて[[太鼓]]や[[鉦]]などの楽器を奏でるものである。「下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞」として[[昭和]]58年（[[1983年]]）5月にいなべ市指定[[無形民俗文化財]]に登録された。

== 由緒 == [[勧請]]年月等不明。古くは「伊原宮」と称した<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』。</ref>。『員弁郡郷土資料』は当神社を「石神社」として[[式内社]]である「[[伊勢国]][[員弁郡]]石神社」に比定して祭神を少名彦命とし、境内に夜ごとに光を発する霊石が存し、これを敬った[[春澄善縄]]が社を建立して「石神社」と称したとの伝えを記す<ref>員弁郡役所編、『員弁郡郷土資料』、[[大正]]4年（1915年）刊。</ref>。『伊勢輯雑記』に、白瀬七郷の[[惣社]]として往時は大社の結構であったと記し、『員弁雑誌』は現下野尻、西野尻両町の[[産土神]]であるとし、これも「往古は壮大なる社なりし」と記す<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』は員弁郡の人である松宮祐重（[[天明]]7年（1787年） - [[弘化]]2年（1845年））の著。『員弁雑誌』は岡田静堂が明治初年に、『伊勢輯雑記』中から員弁郡の部を訂正増補して著録しもの。</ref>。[[文政]]7年（[[1824年]]）の「村明細帳」には「春日大明神」と記され、[[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）の「村明細帳」には「石原神社」とされており<ref>2つの「明細帳」はともに[[徳川林政史研究所]]所蔵。明治の「明細帳」に見える「石原」は「伊原」の誤記である可能性がある（『三重県の地名』）。</ref>、明治45年に村内の八天宮、水神、山神、塞神という小祠を[[合祀]]した<ref>文政明細帳は村内小祠として「愛宕、山神二」を挙げ、明治明細帳は「山神二、水神、走井神」を挙げる。また、『員弁雑誌』は八天宮を「八天狗宮」と、愛宕は「祠なし」と紹介する。</ref>。
== 祭祀 == 明治以前は8月14日が祭日であった（『員弁雑誌』）。『員弁郡郷土資料』によれば、祭礼に際して[[氏子]]は白石を捧げた後に「投石の神事」があったが、[[応永|応永年中]]（14世紀末から15世紀前葉）にそれに事寄せて[[石合戦]]が行われたために禁止されたと伝える<ref>前掲注「石祭投石の事」に、石を社前に捧げるのは「石に留座する」祭神との関係で理解できるが、投石の謂われはなく、危険なので、神役人の他は禁止する旨が記されている。</ref>。
=== 獅子舞 === 10月9日、10日の両日行われ、[[平成]]22年（[[2010年]]）時点で300年以上の歴史があるといわれる。現在使われている獅子頭は[[嘉永]]2年（[[1849年]]）に作られた貴重なもの。[[獅子舞]]は[[獅子]]と[[天狗]]役で演じられ、獅子は春と秋を表す「狂乱牡丹の舞」と「菊の舞」の2つの舞を、天狗は獅子に寄り添って舞の案内や手助けをする。獅子の舞は四方から襲ってくる災厄を追い払いながら[[グルーミング|毛繕い]]をしたり眠ったりと、動物の自然の仕草を取り入れた素朴な舞で、豊作と地域の安全を祈願する。また[[囃子]]（はやし）も奏されるが、これは[[絣]]の着物に花笠をつけた子どもたちが、大人の笛に合わせて[[太鼓]]や[[鉦]]などの楽器を奏でるものである。「下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞」として[[昭和]]58年（[[1983年]]）5月にいなべ市指定[[無形民俗文化財]]に登録された。
==文化財== *下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *『三重県神社誌』、三重県神社庁、平成5年 *『角川日本地名大辞典 三重県』（角川日本地名大辞典第24巻）、[[角川書店]]、昭和58年 *『三重県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系24巻）、[[平凡社]]、昭和58年
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm 三重県の無形民俗文化財一覧]（三重県教育委員会）

{{神社 |名称 = 春日神社 |画像={{画像募集中|cat=いなべ市}} |所在地 = [[三重県]][[いなべ市]]藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |祭神 = 少名彦命 他10柱 |社格 = 旧村社 |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = |別名 = |札所 = |例祭 = 10月8日 |神事 = 奉納獅子舞（10月9・10日） }}
'''春日神社'''（かすがじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[いなべ市]]に鎮座する[[神社]]。[[旧社格]]は[[村社]]。
== 祭神 == [[少名彦命]]、[[大山祇神]]、[[火産霊神]]、[[八衢比古神]]、[[八衢比売神]]、[[岐の神|久那戸神（くなどのかみ）]]、[[磐長姫命]]、[[武甕槌神]]、[[天児屋根命]]、[[経津主命]]、[[罔象女神]]の11柱を祀る。
祭神中、少名彦命は「石に御霊を留座する神」であるという<ref>応永12年8月10日付「石神社石祭投石の事」（『三重県神社誌』所引）。</ref>。
== 由緒 == [[勧請]]年月等不明。古くは「伊原宮」と称した<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』。</ref>。『員弁郡郷土資料』は当神社を「石神社」として[[式内社]]である「[[伊勢国]][[員弁郡]]石神社」に比定して祭神を少名彦命とし、境内に夜ごとに光を発する霊石が存し、これを敬った[[春澄善縄]]が社を建立して「石神社」と称したとの伝えを記す<ref>員弁郡役所編、『員弁郡郷土資料』、[[大正]]4年（1915年）刊。</ref>。『伊勢輯雑記』に、白瀬七郷の[[惣社]]として往時は大社の結構であったと記し、『員弁雑誌』は現下野尻、西野尻両町の[[産土神]]であるとし、これも「往古は壮大なる社なりし」と記す<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』は員弁郡の人である松宮祐重（[[天明]]7年（1787年） - [[弘化]]2年（1845年））の著。『員弁雑誌』は岡田静堂が明治初年に、『伊勢輯雑記』中から員弁郡の部を訂正増補して著録しもの。</ref>。[[文政]]7年（[[1824年]]）の「村明細帳」には「春日大明神」と記され、[[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）の「村明細帳」には「石原神社」とされており<ref>2つの「明細帳」はともに[[徳川林政史研究所]]所蔵。明治の「明細帳」に見える「石原」は「伊原」の誤記である可能性がある（『三重県の地名』）。</ref>、明治45年に村内の八天宮、水神、山神、塞神という小祠を[[合祀]]した<ref>文政明細帳は村内小祠として「愛宕、山神二」を挙げ、明治明細帳は「山神二、水神、走井神」を挙げる。また、『員弁雑誌』は八天宮を「八天狗宮」と、愛宕は「祠なし」と紹介する。</ref>。
== 祭祀 == 明治以前は8月14日が祭日であった（『員弁雑誌』）。『員弁郡郷土資料』によれば、祭礼に際して[[氏子]]は白石を捧げた後に「投石の神事」があったが、[[応永|応永年中]]（14世紀末から15世紀前葉）にそれに事寄せて[[石合戦]]が行われたために禁止されたと伝える<ref>前掲注「石祭投石の事」に、石を社前に捧げるのは「石に留座する」祭神との関係で理解できるが、投石の謂われはなく、危険なので、神役人の他は禁止する旨が記されている。</ref>。
=== 獅子舞 === 10月9日、10日の両日行われ、[[平成]]22年（[[2010年]]）時点で300年以上の歴史があるといわれる。現在使われている獅子頭は[[嘉永]]2年（[[1849年]]）に作られた貴重なもの。[[獅子舞]]は[[獅子]]と[[天狗]]役で演じられ、獅子は春と秋を表す「狂乱牡丹の舞」と「菊の舞」の2つの舞を、天狗は獅子に寄り添って舞の案内や手助けをする。獅子の舞は四方から襲ってくる災厄を追い払いながら[[グルーミング|毛繕い]]をしたり眠ったりと、動物の自然の仕草を取り入れた素朴な舞で、豊作と地域の安全を祈願する。また[[囃子]]（はやし）も奏されるが、これは[[絣]]の着物に花笠をつけた子どもたちが、大人の笛に合わせて[[太鼓]]や[[鉦]]などの楽器を奏でるものである。「下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞」として[[昭和]]58年（[[1983年]]）5月にいなべ市指定[[無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
==文化財== *下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞

祭神中、少名彦命は「石に御霊を留座する神」であるという<ref>応永12年8月10日付「石神社石祭投石の事」（『三重県神社誌』所引）。</ref>。
== 由緒 == [[勧請]]年月等不明。古くは「伊原宮」と称した<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』。</ref>。『員弁郡郷土資料』は当神社を「石神社」として[[式内社]]である「[[伊勢国]][[員弁郡]]石神社」に比定して祭神を少名彦命とし、境内に夜ごとに光を発する霊石が存し、これを敬った[[春澄善縄]]が社を建立して「石神社」と称したとの伝えを記す<ref>員弁郡役所編、『員弁郡郷土資料』、[[大正]]4年（1915年）刊。</ref>。『伊勢輯雑記』に、白瀬七郷の[[惣社]]として往時は大社の結構であったと記し、『員弁雑誌』は現下野尻、西野尻両町の[[産土神]]であるとし、これも「往古は壮大なる社なりし」と記す<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』は員弁郡の人である松宮祐重（[[天明]]7年（1787年） - [[弘化]]2年（1845年））の著。『員弁雑誌』は岡田静堂が明治初年に、『伊勢輯雑記』中から員弁郡の部を訂正増補して著録しもの。</ref>。[[文政]]7年（[[1824年]]）の「村明細帳」には「春日大明神」と記され、[[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）の「村明細帳」には「石原神社」とされており<ref>2つの「明細帳」はともに[[徳川林政史研究所]]所蔵。明治の「明細帳」に見える「石原」は「伊原」の誤記である可能性がある（『三重県の地名』）。</ref>、明治45年に村内の八天宮、水神、山神、塞神という小祠を[[合祀]]した<ref>文政明細帳は村内小祠として「愛宕、山神二」を挙げ、明治明細帳は「山神二、水神、走井神」を挙げる。また、『員弁雑誌』は八天宮を「八天狗宮」と、愛宕は「祠なし」と紹介する。</ref>。
== 祭祀 == 明治以前は8月14日が祭日であった（『員弁雑誌』）。『員弁郡郷土資料』によれば、祭礼に際して[[氏子]]は白石を捧げた後に「投石の神事」があったが、[[応永|応永年中]]（14世紀末から15世紀前葉）にそれに事寄せて[[石合戦]]が行われたために禁止されたと伝える<ref>前掲注「石祭投石の事」に、石を社前に捧げるのは「石に留座する」祭神との関係で理解できるが、投石の謂われはなく、危険なので、神役人の他は禁止する旨が記されている。</ref>。
=== 獅子舞 === 10月9日、10日の両日行われ、[[平成]]22年（[[2010年]]）時点で300年以上の歴史があるといわれる。現在使われている獅子頭は[[嘉永]]2年（[[1849年]]）に作られた貴重なもの。[[獅子舞]]は[[獅子]]と[[天狗]]役で演じられ、獅子は春と秋を表す「狂乱牡丹の舞」と「菊の舞」の2つの舞を、天狗は獅子に寄り添って舞の案内や手助けをする。獅子の舞は四方から襲ってくる災厄を追い払いながら[[グルーミング|毛繕い]]をしたり眠ったりと、動物の自然の仕草を取り入れた素朴な舞で、豊作と地域の安全を祈願する。また[[囃子]]（はやし）も奏されるが、これは[[絣]]の着物に花笠をつけた子どもたちが、大人の笛に合わせて[[太鼓]]や[[鉦]]などの楽器を奏でるものである。「下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞」として[[昭和]]58年（[[1983年]]）5月にいなべ市指定[[無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
==文化財== *下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *『三重県神社誌』、三重県神社庁、平成5年 *『角川日本地名大辞典 三重県』（角川日本地名大辞典第24巻）、[[角川書店]]、昭和58年 *『三重県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系24巻）、[[平凡社]]、昭和58年
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm 三重県の無形民俗文化財一覧]（三重県教育委員会）

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 春日神社 |画像 = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |所在地 = [[三重県]][[いなべ市]]藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |祭神 = 少名彦命 他10柱 |社格 = 旧村社 |創建 = 不明 |本殿 = |別名 = |札所 = |例祭 = 10月8日 |神事 = 奉納獅子舞（10月9・10日） }}
'''春日神社'''（かすがじんじゃ）は、[[三重県]][[いなべ市]]に鎮座する[[神社]]。[[旧社格]]は[[村社]]。
== 祭神 == [[少名彦命]]、[[大山祇神]]、[[火産霊神]]、[[八衢比古神]]、[[八衢比売神]]、[[岐の神|久那戸神（くなどのかみ）]]、[[磐長姫命]]、[[武甕槌神]]、[[天児屋根命]]、[[経津主命]]、[[罔象女神]]の11柱を祀る。
祭神中、少名彦命は「石に御霊を留座する神」であるという<ref>応永12年8月10日付「石神社石祭投石の事」（『三重県神社誌』所引）。</ref>。
== 由緒 == [[勧請]]年月等不明。古くは「伊原宮」と称した<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』。</ref>。『員弁郡郷土資料』は当神社を「石神社」として[[式内社]]である「[[伊勢国]][[員弁郡]]石神社」に比定して祭神を少名彦命とし、境内に夜ごとに光を発する霊石が存し、これを敬った[[春澄善縄]]が社を建立して「石神社」と称したとの伝えを記す<ref>員弁郡役所編、『員弁郡郷土資料』、[[大正]]4年（1915年）刊。</ref>。『伊勢輯雑記』に、白瀬七郷の[[惣社]]として往時は大社の結構であったと記し、『員弁雑誌』は現下野尻、西野尻両町の[[産土神]]であるとし、これも「往古は壮大なる社なりし」と記す<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』は員弁郡の人である松宮祐重（[[天明]]7年（1787年） - [[弘化]]2年（1845年））の著。『員弁雑誌』は岡田静堂が明治初年に、『伊勢輯雑記』中から員弁郡の部を訂正増補して著録しもの。</ref>。[[文政]]7年（[[1824年]]）の「村明細帳」には「春日大明神」と記され、[[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）の「村明細帳」には「石原神社」とされており<ref>2つの「明細帳」はともに[[徳川林政史研究所]]所蔵。明治の「明細帳」に見える「石原」は「伊原」の誤記である可能性がある（『三重県の地名』）。</ref>、明治45年に村内の八天宮、水神、山神、塞神という小祠を[[合祀]]した<ref>文政明細帳は村内小祠として「愛宕、山神二」を挙げ、明治明細帳は「山神二、水神、走井神」を挙げる。また、『員弁雑誌』は八天宮を「八天狗宮」と、愛宕は「祠なし」と紹介する。</ref>。
== 祭祀 == 明治以前は8月14日が祭日であった（『員弁雑誌』）。『員弁郡郷土資料』によれば、祭礼に際して[[氏子]]は白石を捧げた後に「投石の神事」があったが、[[応永|応永年中]]（14世紀末から15世紀前葉）にそれに事寄せて[[石合戦]]が行われたために禁止されたと伝える<ref>前掲注「石祭投石の事」に、石を社前に捧げるのは「石に留座する」祭神との関係で理解できるが、投石の謂われはなく、危険なので、神役人の他は禁止する旨が記されている。</ref>。
=== 獅子舞 === 10月9日、10日の両日行われ、[[平成]]22年（[[2010年]]）時点で300年以上の歴史があるといわれる。現在使われている獅子頭は[[嘉永]]2年（[[1849年]]）に作られた貴重なもの。[[獅子舞]]は[[唐獅子|獅子]]と[[天狗]]役で演じられ、獅子は春と秋を表す「狂乱牡丹の舞」と「菊の舞」の2つの舞を、天狗は獅子に寄り添って舞の案内や手助けをする。獅子の舞は四方から襲ってくる災厄を追い払いながら[[グルーミング|毛繕い]]をしたり眠ったりと、動物の自然の仕草を取り入れた素朴な舞で、豊作と地域の安全を祈願する。また[[囃子]]（はやし）も奏されるが、これは[[絣]]の着物に花笠をつけた子どもたちが、大人の笛に合わせて[[太鼓]]や[[鉦]]などの楽器を奏でるものである。「下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞」として[[昭和]]58年（[[1983年]]）5月にいなべ市指定[[無形民俗文化財]]に指定された。
==文化財== *下野尻春日神社奉納獅子舞

revision.text:
{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |Image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市}}. Its former shrine rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22年}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== 脚注 == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, Shōwa 58
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm List of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Mie Prefecture] (Mie Prefectural Board of Education)

The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22年}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== 脚注 == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |Image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市}}. Its former shrine rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.

In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year }} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== Footnote == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, Shōwa 58
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm List of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Mie Prefecture] (Mie Prefectural Board of Education)

revision.text:
{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |Image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市}}. Its former shrine rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year }} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== Footnote == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, Shōwa 58
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm List of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Mie Prefecture] (Mie Prefectural Board of Education)

In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|12=draft}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=draft}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=draft}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|12=draft}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|12=draft}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|12=draft}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|12=draft}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|12=draft}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|12=draft}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|12=draft}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|12=draft}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|12=draft}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|12=draft}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== Footnote == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店|12=draft}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}, Shōwa 58

revision.text:
{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|12=draft|12=draft}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|12=draft|12=draft}}. Its former shrine rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=draft|12=draft}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|12=draft|12=draft}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft|12=draft}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡|12=draft|12=draft}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=draft|12=draft}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=draft|12=draft}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|12=draft|12=draft}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|12=draft|12=draft}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=draft|12=draft}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=draft|12=draft}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|12=draft|12=draft}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|12=draft|12=draft}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|12=draft|12=draft}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|12=draft|12=draft}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|12=draft|12=draft}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|12=draft|12=draft}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|12=draft|12=draft}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|12=draft|12=draft}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|12=draft|12=draft}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft|12=draft}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|12=draft|12=draft}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}

== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=draft|12=draft}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|12=draft|12=draft}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|12=draft|12=draft}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡|12=draft|12=draft}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=draft|12=draft}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=draft|12=draft}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|12=draft|12=draft}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|12=draft|12=draft}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=draft|12=draft}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=draft|12=draft}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|12=draft|12=draft}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|12=draft|12=draft}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|12=draft|12=draft}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|12=draft|12=draft}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|12=draft|12=draft}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|12=draft|12=draft}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|12=draft|12=draft}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|12=draft|12=draft}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|12=draft|12=draft}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft|12=draft}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|12=draft|12=draft}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}

revision.text:
{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|12=draft|12=draft}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|12=draft|12=draft}}. Its former shrine rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=draft|12=draft}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|12=draft|12=draft}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡|12=draft|12=draft}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=draft|12=draft}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=draft|12=draft}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|12=draft|12=draft}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|12=draft|12=draft}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=draft|12=draft}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=draft|12=draft}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|12=draft|12=draft}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|12=draft|12=draft}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|12=draft|12=draft}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|12=draft|12=draft}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|12=draft|12=draft}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|12=draft|12=draft}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|12=draft|12=draft}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|12=draft|12=draft}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|12=draft|12=draft}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft|12=draft}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance

== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店|12=draft|12=draft}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft|12=draft}}, Shōwa 58

In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|12=draft|12=draft}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=draft|12=draft}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=draft|12=draft}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|12=draft|12=draft}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|12=draft|12=draft}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|12=draft|12=draft}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|12=draft|12=draft}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|12=draft|12=draft}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|12=draft|12=draft}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|12=draft|12=draft}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|12=draft|12=draft}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|12=draft|12=draft}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft|12=draft}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== Footnote == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店|12=draft|12=draft}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft|12=draft}}, Shōwa 58
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm List of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Mie Prefecture] (Mie Prefectural Board of Education)

revision.text:
{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|12=draft|12=draft}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|12=draft|12=draft}}. Its former shrine rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=draft|12=draft}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|12=draft|12=draft}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡|12=draft|12=draft}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=draft|12=draft}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=draft|12=draft}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|12=draft|12=draft}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|12=draft|12=draft}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=draft|12=draft}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=draft|12=draft}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|12=draft|12=draft}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|12=draft|12=draft}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|12=draft|12=draft}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|12=draft|12=draft}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|12=draft|12=draft}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|12=draft|12=draft}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|12=draft|12=draft}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|12=draft|12=draft}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|12=draft|12=draft}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft|12=draft}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== Footnote == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店|12=draft|12=draft}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft|12=draft}}, Shōwa 58
== 外部リンク == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm List of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Mie Prefecture] (Mie Prefectural Board of Education)
