{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|12=draft|12=draft}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|12=draft|12=draft}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=draft|12=draft}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|12=draft|12=draft}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡|12=draft|12=draft}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=draft|12=draft}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=draft|12=draft}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|12=draft|12=draft}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|12=draft|12=draft}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|12=draft|12=draft}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=draft|12=draft}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=draft|12=draft}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|12=draft|12=draft}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|12=draft|12=draft}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|12=draft|12=draft}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|12=draft|12=draft}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|12=draft|12=draft}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|12=draft|12=draft}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|12=draft|12=draft}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|12=draft|12=draft}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|12=draft|12=draft}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft|12=draft}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== Footnote == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店|12=draft|12=draft}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft|12=draft}}, Shōwa 58
== External links == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm List of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Mie Prefecture] (Mie Prefectural Board of Education)

According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=draft|12=draft}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|12=draft|12=draft}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|12=draft|12=draft}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|12=draft|12=draft}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|12=draft|12=draft}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|12=draft|12=draft}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|12=draft|12=draft}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|12=draft|12=draft}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|12=draft|12=draft}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|12=draft|12=draft}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft|12=draft}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance

{{draft categories| [[Category:Mie Prefecture Shrines]] [[Category:Kasuga Shrine]] [[Category:Former Village Shrines of Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Inabe City]] [[Category:Buildings in Inabe City]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Iwanaga Faith]] [[Category:New]] }}
[[ja:春日神社]]

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|12=draft}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|12=draft}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|12=draft}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagutsuchi|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=draft}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|12=draft}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡|12=draft}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=draft}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=draft}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|12=draft}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|12=draft}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|12=draft}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=draft}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=draft}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|12=draft}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|12=draft}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|12=draft}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|12=draft}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|12=draft}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|12=draft}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|12=draft}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|12=draft}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|12=draft}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|12=draft}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|12=draft}} 58 (1983).

[[ja:春日神社]]

revision.comment: 118 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:春日神社 up to rev 104041070
revision.text:
{{otheruses|各地の「春日神社」を神社名とする神社|春日神を祀る神社全般|春日神}} '''春日神社'''（かすがじんじゃ）は、｢春日」を社名に持ち[[春日神]]を祭神とする神社。全国に約1,000社あり、奈良県奈良市の[[春日大社]]を総本社とする。
ここでは「春日神社」を法人名とする神社を記す（神社名の一部に「春日」を含むものについては「[[:Category:春日神社]]」を参照）。
== 主な春日神社 == === 東北地方 === * [[春日神社 (仙台市青葉区)]]（[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[青葉区 (仙台市)|青葉区]]春日町） * [[春日神社 (米沢市)]]（[[山形県]][[米沢市]]丸の内） * [[春日神社 (鶴岡市黒川宮の下)]]（山形県[[鶴岡市]]黒川宮の下） * [[春日神社 (福島市岡部)]]（[[福島県]][[福島市]]岡部） * [[春日神社 (福島市渡利)]]（福島県福島市渡利） * [[春日神社 (郡山市大槻町)]]（福島県[[郡山市]]大槻町） - 大槻春日神社 * [[春日神社 (川俣町宮前)]]（福島県[[伊達郡]][[川俣町]]宮前）

=== 四国地方 === * [[春日神社 (徳島市)]]（[[徳島県]][[徳島市]][[眉山町]]） * [[春日神社 (徳島市下助任町)]]（徳島県徳島市[[下助任町]]） * [[春日神社 (藍住町)]]（徳島県[[板野郡]][[藍住町]]矢上） * [[中喜来春日神社]]（徳島県板野郡[[松茂町]]中喜来） * [[春日神社 (坂出市)]]（[[香川県]][[坂出市]]川津町） * [[春日神社 (丸亀市)]]（香川県[[丸亀市]]川西町） – 丸亀春日神社 * [[春日神社 (善通寺市原田町)]]（香川県[[善通寺市]]原田町） * [[春日神社 (善通寺市弘田町)]]（香川県善通寺市弘田町） * [[春日神社 (香川県琴平町)]]（香川県[[仲多度郡]][[琴平町]]榎井） * [[春日神社 (今治市)]]（[[愛媛県]][[今治市]]国分） * [[春日神社 (東洋町)]]（[[高知県]][[安芸郡 (高知県)|安芸郡]][[東洋町]]野根） * [[春日神社 (高知県大月町)]]（高知県[[幡多郡]][[大月町]]一切） * [[春日神社 (佐川町)]]（高知県[[高岡郡]][[佐川町]]甲）
=== 九州地方 === * [[春日神社 (北九州市)]]（[[福岡県]][[北九州市]][[八幡西区]]藤田） * [[春日神社 (北九州市門司区)]]（福岡県北九州市[[門司区]]田野浦） * [[春日神社 (田川市)]]（福岡県[[田川市]]宮尾町） * [[春日神社 (春日市)]]（福岡県[[春日市]]春日） * [[春日神社 (福智町)]]（福岡県[[田川郡]][[福智町]]市場） * [[春日神社 (唐津市)]]（[[佐賀県]][[唐津市]]養母田） * [[春日神社 (佐世保市吉井町)]]（[[長崎県]][[佐世保市]]吉井町） * [[春日神社 (佐世保市春日町)]]（長崎県佐世保市春日町） * [[春日神社 (熊本市)]]（[[熊本県]][[熊本市]][[西区 (熊本市)|西区]]春日） * [[春日神社 (大分市)]]（[[大分県]][[大分市]]勢家町） * [[春日神社 (豊後高田市)]]（大分県[[豊後高田市]]草地） * [[春日神社 (延岡市)]]（[[宮崎県]][[延岡市]]恒富町） * [[春日神社 (都城市高城町)]]（宮崎県[[都城市]]高城町） * [[春日神社 (鹿児島市)]]（[[鹿児島県]][[鹿児島市]][[春日町 (鹿児島市)|春日町]]） * [[春日神社 (日置市東市来町)]]（鹿児島県[[日置市]]東市来町） * [[春日神社 (姶良市)]]（鹿児島県[[姶良市]]加治木町） * [[春日神社 (鹿屋市)]]（鹿児島県[[鹿屋市]]打馬）

revision.text:
{{otheruses|各地の「春日神社」を神社名とする神社|春日神を祀る神社全般|春日神}} '''春日神社'''（かすがじんじゃ）は、｢春日」を社名に持ち[[春日神]]を祭神とする神社。全国に約1,000社あり、奈良県奈良市の[[春日大社]]を総本社とする。
ここでは「春日神社」を法人名とする神社を記す（神社名の一部に「春日」を含むものについては「[[:Category:春日神社]]」を参照）。
== 主な春日神社 == === 東北地方 === * [[春日神社 (仙台市青葉区)]]（[[宮城県]][[仙台市]][[青葉区 (仙台市)|青葉区]]春日町） * [[春日神社 (米沢市)]]（[[山形県]][[米沢市]]丸の内） * [[春日神社 (鶴岡市黒川宮の下)]]（山形県[[鶴岡市]]黒川宮の下） * [[春日神社 (福島市岡部)]]（[[福島県]][[福島市]]岡部） * [[春日神社 (福島市渡利)]]（福島県福島市渡利） * [[春日神社 (郡山市大槻町)]]（福島県[[郡山市]]大槻町） - 大槻春日神社 * [[春日神社 (川俣町宮前)]]（福島県[[伊達郡]][[川俣町]]宮前）
=== 関東地方 === * [[春日神社 (常陸太田市高貫町)]]（[[茨城県]][[常陸太田市]]高貫町） * [[春日神社 (那珂市)]]（茨城県[[那珂市]]福田） * [[春日神社 (高崎市)]]（[[群馬県]][[高崎市]]上里見町） * [[春日神社 (入間市)]]（[[埼玉県]][[入間市]]春日町） * [[春日神社 (蕨市)]]（埼玉県[[蕨市]]錦町） * [[春日神社 (上尾市)]]（埼玉県[[上尾市]][[柏座]]） * [[春日神社 (越生町)]]（埼玉県[[入間郡]][[越生町]]西和田） * [[春日神社 (松戸市)]]（[[千葉県]][[松戸市]][[紙敷]]） * [[八幡春日神社]]（千葉県[[鎌ケ谷市]][[中沢 (鎌ケ谷市)|中沢]]） * [[三田春日神社]]（[[東京都]][[港区 (東京都)|港区]][[三田 (東京都港区)|三田]]） * [[春日神社 (杉並区)]]（東京都[[杉並区]][[宮前 (杉並区)|宮前]]） * [[春日神社 (大田区)]]（東京都[[大田区]][[中央 (大田区)|中央]]） * [[春日神社 (練馬区)]]（東京都[[練馬区]][[春日町 (練馬区)|春日町]]） * [[春日神社 (八王子市川口町)]]（東京都[[八王子市]][[川口町 (八王子市)|川口町]]） * [[春日神社 (青梅市野上町)]]（東京都[[青梅市]][[野上町 (青梅市)|野上町]]） * [[春日神社 (青梅市河辺町)]]（東京都青梅市[[河辺町 (青梅市)|河辺町]]） * [[春日神社 (日の出町)]]（東京都[[西多摩郡]][[日の出町]]平井） * [[春日神社 (大島町)]]（東京都[[大島町]]差木地） * [[春日神社 (横浜市)]]（[[神奈川県]][[横浜市]][[港南区]][[日野中央]]） - 日野春日神社 * [[相州春日神社]]（神奈川県横浜市[[戸塚区]]俣野町） * [[春日神社 (川崎市)]]（神奈川県[[川崎市]][[中原区]][[宮内 (川崎市)|宮内]]） * [[春日神社 (横須賀市)]]（神奈川県[[横須賀市]][[三春町 (横須賀市)|三春町]]） - 三春春日神社 * [[春日神社 (平塚市)]]（神奈川県[[平塚市]]平塚） * [[春日神社 (厚木市)]]（神奈川県[[厚木市]]温水）

'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagutsuchi|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance

== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, Shōwa 58

This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== Footnote == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Shōwa 58
== External links == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm List of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Mie Prefecture] (Mie Prefectural Board of Education)

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagutsuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 58 (1983).

In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei era|qq=}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Battle|qq=}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei period|qq=}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaei era|qq=}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lion Dance|qq=}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese Lion|qq=}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu|qq=}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grooming|qq=}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashi|qq=}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiko|qq=}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bell|qq=}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa period|qq=}} 58 (1983).

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefecture|qq=}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabe City|qq=}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefecture|qq=}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabe City|qq=}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagutsuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunado no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|qq=}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enshrinement|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjō|qq=}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabe District|qq=}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|qq=}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|qq=}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutelary Deity|13=User:Immanuelle/Ubusunagami|qq=}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunsei period|qq=}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|qq=}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei era|qq=}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Battle|qq=}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei period|qq=}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaei era|qq=}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lion Dance|qq=}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese Lion|qq=}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu|qq=}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grooming|qq=}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashi|qq=}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiko|qq=}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bell|qq=}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa period|qq=}} 58 (1983).

The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabe District|qq=}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|qq=}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|qq=}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutelary Deity|13=User:Immanuelle/Ubusunagami|qq=}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunsei period|qq=}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|qq=}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei era|qq=}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Battle|qq=}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei period|qq=}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaei era|qq=}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lion Dance|qq=}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese Lion|qq=}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu|qq=}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grooming|qq=}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashi|qq=}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiko|qq=}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bell|qq=}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa period|qq=}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefecture|qq=}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabe City|qq=}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefecture|qq=}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabe City|qq=}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunado no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|qq=}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enshrinement|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjō|qq=}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabe District|qq=}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|qq=}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|qq=}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutelary Deity|13=User:Immanuelle/Ubusunagami|qq=}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunsei period|qq=}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|qq=}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei era|qq=}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Battle|qq=}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei period|qq=}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaei era|qq=}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lion Dance|qq=}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese Lion|qq=}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu|qq=}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grooming|qq=}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashi|qq=}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiko|qq=}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bell|qq=}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa period|qq=}} 58 (1983).

'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefecture|qq=}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabe City|qq=}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunado no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|qq=}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enshrinement|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjō|qq=}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabe District|qq=}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|qq=}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|qq=}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutelary Deity|13=User:Immanuelle/Ubusunagami|qq=}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunsei period|qq=}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|qq=}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei era|qq=}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Battle|qq=}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22nd year|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei period|qq=}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaei era|qq=}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lion Dance|qq=}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese Lion|qq=}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu|qq=}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grooming|qq=}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashi|qq=}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiko|qq=}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bell|qq=}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa period|qq=}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance

== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Shoten|qq=}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha|qq=}}, Shōwa 58

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefecture|qq=}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabe City|qq=}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
