[[ja:春日神社]]

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefecture|qq=}}{{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabe City|qq=}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mie Prefecture|qq=}} {{ill|Inabe City|ja|いなべ市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabe City|qq=}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Kunado no Kami|ja|岐の神|lt=Kunado no Kami|lt_ja=久那戸神（くなどのかみ）|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunado no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|qq=}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enshrinement|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjō|qq=}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} “{{ill|Ise Province|ja|伊勢国|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Province|qq=}}{{ill|Inabe District|ja|員弁郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inabe District|qq=}} Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|qq=}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|qq=}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Tutelary Deity|ja|産土神|1=Ubusunagami|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tutelary Deity|13=User:Immanuelle/Ubusunagami|qq=}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei period|ja|文政|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunsei period|qq=}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|1=Meiji era|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|qq=}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei era|ja|応永|lt=Ōei era|lt_ja=応永年中|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōei era|qq=}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Stone Battle|ja|石合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Stone Battle|qq=}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei period|ja|平成|lt=Heisei 22|lt_ja=22年|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei period|qq=}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei era|ja|嘉永|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaei era|qq=}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion Dance|ja|獅子舞|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lion Dance|qq=}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese Lion|ja|唐獅子|lt=Lion|lt_ja=獅子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese Lion|qq=}} and {{ill|Tengu|ja|天狗|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tengu|qq=}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Grooming|ja|グルーミング|lt=Fur Care|lt_ja=毛繕い|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Grooming|qq=}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi|ja|囃子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayashi|qq=}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Taiko|ja|太鼓|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiko|qq=}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Bell|ja|鉦|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bell|qq=}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa period|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa period|qq=}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== Footnote == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kadokawa Shoten|qq=}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heibonsha|qq=}}, Shōwa 58
== External links == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm List of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Mie Prefecture] (Mie Prefectural Board of Education)

'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] [[:en:Inabe|Inabe City]]. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|qq=}}, [[:en:Kunado-no-Kami|Kunado no Kami]], {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|qq=}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enshrinement|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjō|qq=}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} “[[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]][[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|qq=}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|qq=}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ([[:en:Ubusunagami|Ubusunagami]]) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|qq=}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the [[:en:Ōei|Ōei era]] (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the [[:en:Seokjeon|Stone Battle]] and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of [[:en:Heisei era|Heisei 22]] (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the [[:en:Kaei|Kaei era]] (1849). The [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]] is enacted with roles by the [[:en:Chinese guardian lions|Lion]] and [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]]; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ([[:en:Personal grooming|Fur Care]]) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ([[:en:Hayashi (music)|Hayashi]]) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ([[:en:Drum|Taiko]]) and cymbals ([[:en:Kane (instrument)|Bell]]) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by Inabe City in May of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa period]] 58 (1983).

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]][[:en:Inabe|Inabe City]]藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] [[:en:Inabe|Inabe City]]. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|qq=}}, [[:en:Kunado-no-Kami|Kunado no Kami]], {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|qq=}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enshrinement|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjō|qq=}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} “[[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]][[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|qq=}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|qq=}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ([[:en:Ubusunagami|Ubusunagami]]) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|qq=}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the [[:en:Ōei|Ōei era]] (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the [[:en:Seokjeon|Stone Battle]] and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of [[:en:Heisei era|Heisei 22]] (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the [[:en:Kaei|Kaei era]] (1849). The [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]] is enacted with roles by the [[:en:Chinese guardian lions|Lion]] and [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]]; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ([[:en:Personal grooming|Fur Care]]) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ([[:en:Hayashi (music)|Hayashi]]) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ([[:en:Drum|Taiko]]) and cymbals ([[:en:Kane (instrument)|Bell]]) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by Inabe City in May of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa period]] 58 (1983).

== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enshrinement|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjō|qq=}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} “[[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]][[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|qq=}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|qq=}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ([[:en:Ubusunagami|Ubusunagami]]) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|qq=}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the [[:en:Ōei|Ōei era]] (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the [[:en:Seokjeon|Stone Battle]] and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of [[:en:Heisei era|Heisei 22]] (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the [[:en:Kaei|Kaei era]] (1849). The [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]] is enacted with roles by the [[:en:Chinese guardian lions|Lion]] and [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]]; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ([[:en:Personal grooming|Fur Care]]) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ([[:en:Hayashi (music)|Hayashi]]) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ([[:en:Drum|Taiko]]) and cymbals ([[:en:Kane (instrument)|Bell]]) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by Inabe City in May of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa period]] 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== Footnote == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), [[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), [[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], Shōwa 58
== External links == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm List of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Mie Prefecture] (Mie Prefectural Board of Education)

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]][[:en:Inabe|Inabe City]]藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] [[:en:Inabe|Inabe City]]. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|qq=}}, [[:en:Kunado-no-Kami|Kunado no Kami]], {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|qq=}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|qq=}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|qq=}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|qq=}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Enshrinement|ja|勧請|lt=Enshrinement|1=Kanjō|2=ja|3=分霊|4=es|5=Bunrei|6=id|7=Kanjō|8=zh|9=分靈|10=fr|11=Kanjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enshrinement|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjō|qq=}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=}} “[[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]][[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|qq=}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|qq=}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ([[:en:Ubusunagami|Ubusunagami]]) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|qq=}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the [[:en:Ōei|Ōei era]] (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the [[:en:Seokjeon|Stone Battle]] and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of [[:en:Heisei era|Heisei 22]] (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the [[:en:Kaei|Kaei era]] (1849). The [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]] is enacted with roles by the [[:en:Chinese guardian lions|Lion]] and [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]]; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ([[:en:Personal grooming|Fur Care]]) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ([[:en:Hayashi (music)|Hayashi]]) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ([[:en:Drum|Taiko]]) and cymbals ([[:en:Kane (instrument)|Bell]]) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by Inabe City in May of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa period]] 58 (1983).

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]][[:en:Inabe|Inabe City]]藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}

According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the [[:en:Ōei|Ōei era]] (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the [[:en:Seokjeon|Stone Battle]] and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of [[:en:Heisei era|Heisei 22]] (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the [[:en:Kaei|Kaei era]] (1849). The [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]] is enacted with roles by the [[:en:Chinese guardian lions|Lion]] and [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]]; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ([[:en:Personal grooming|Fur Care]]) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ([[:en:Hayashi (music)|Hayashi]]) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ([[:en:Drum|Taiko]]) and cymbals ([[:en:Kane (instrument)|Bell]]) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=}} by Inabe City in May of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa period]] 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance

[[ja:春日神社]] [[Category:Shrine disambiguations]] [[Category:Kasuga shrines]] [[Category:Disambiguation pages]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:가스가 신사]] [[zh:春日神社]]

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]][[:en:Inabe|Inabe City]]藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] [[:en:Inabe|Inabe City]]. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岐の神}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岐の神}}, [[:en:Kunado-no-Kami|Kunado no Kami]], {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イワナガヒメ}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミカヅチ}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 経津主神}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of [[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]] are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} “[[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]][[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|qq=}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総社}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ([[:en:Ubusunagami|Ubusunagami]]) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|qq=}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氏神|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the [[:en:Ōei|Ōei era]] (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the [[:en:Seokjeon|Stone Battle]] and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of [[:en:Heisei era|Heisei 22]] (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the [[:en:Kaei|Kaei era]] (1849). The [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]] is enacted with roles by the [[:en:Chinese guardian lions|Lion]] and [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]]; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ([[:en:Personal grooming|Fur Care]]) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ([[:en:Hayashi (music)|Hayashi]]) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ([[:en:Drum|Taiko]]) and cymbals ([[:en:Kane (instrument)|Bell]]) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財}} by Inabe City in May of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa period]] 58 (1983).

The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} “[[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]][[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ([[:en:Personal grooming|Fur Care]]) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ([[:en:Hayashi (music)|Hayashi]]) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ([[:en:Drum|Taiko]]) and cymbals ([[:en:Kane (instrument)|Bell]]) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財}} by Inabe City in May of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa period]] 58 (1983).

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category History of Inabe City
revision.text:
{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]][[:en:Inabe|Inabe City]]藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] [[:en:Inabe|Inabe City]]. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岐の神}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岐の神}}, [[:en:Kunado-no-Kami|Kunado no Kami]], {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イワナガヒメ}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミカヅチ}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 経津主神}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of [[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]] are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} “[[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]][[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|qq=}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総社}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ([[:en:Ubusunagami|Ubusunagami]]) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|qq=}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氏神|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the [[:en:Ōei|Ōei era]] (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the [[:en:Seokjeon|Stone Battle]] and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of [[:en:Heisei era|Heisei 22]] (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the [[:en:Kaei|Kaei era]] (1849). The [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]] is enacted with roles by the [[:en:Chinese guardian lions|Lion]] and [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]]; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ([[:en:Personal grooming|Fur Care]]) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ([[:en:Hayashi (music)|Hayashi]]) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ([[:en:Drum|Taiko]]) and cymbals ([[:en:Kane (instrument)|Bell]]) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財}} by Inabe City in May of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa period]] 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== Footnote == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), [[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), [[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], Shōwa 58
== External links == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm List of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Mie Prefecture] (Mie Prefectural Board of Education)

revision.text: #REDIRECT [[Kasuga Shrines]]

{{Shinto Shrine |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = [[File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg|300px]] |Location = [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]][[:en:Inabe|Inabe City]]藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] [[:en:Inabe|Inabe City]]. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオヤマツミ}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カグツチ}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岐の神}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 岐の神}}, [[:en:Kunado-no-Kami|Kunado no Kami]], {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イワナガヒメ}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タケミカヅチ}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 経津主神}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ミヅハノメ}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of [[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]] are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 延喜式神名帳}} “[[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]][[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to スクナビコナ}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|qq=}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 総社}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ([[:en:Ubusunagami|Ubusunagami]]) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|qq=}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 氏神|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the [[:en:Ōei|Ōei era]] (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the [[:en:Seokjeon|Stone Battle]] and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of [[:en:Heisei era|Heisei 22]] (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the [[:en:Kaei|Kaei era]] (1849). The [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]] is enacted with roles by the [[:en:Chinese guardian lions|Lion]] and [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]]; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ([[:en:Personal grooming|Fur Care]]) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ([[:en:Hayashi (music)|Hayashi]]) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ([[:en:Drum|Taiko]]) and cymbals ([[:en:Kane (instrument)|Bell]]) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財}} by Inabe City in May of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa period]] 58 (1983).

'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] [[:en:Inabe|Inabe City]]. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to 近代社格制度}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to オオヤマツミ}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to カグツチ}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to 岐の神}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to 岐の神}}, [[:en:Kunado-no-Kami|Kunado no Kami]], {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to イワナガヒメ}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to タケミカヅチ}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to 天児屋命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to 経津主神}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to ミヅハノメ}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to スクナビコナ}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of [[:en:Bunrei|Enshrinement]] are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to 延喜式神名帳}} “[[:en:Ise Province|Ise Province]][[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to スクナビコナ}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|qq=}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to 総社}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ([[:en:Ubusunagami|Ubusunagami]]) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei period]] (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|qq=}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to 氏神|comment=enwiki is a [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the [[:en:Ōei|Ōei era]] (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the [[:en:Seokjeon|Stone Battle]] and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of [[:en:Heisei era|Heisei 22]] (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the [[:en:Kaei|Kaei era]] (1849). The [[:en:Lion dance|Lion Dance]] is enacted with roles by the [[:en:Chinese guardian lions|Lion]] and [[:en:Tengu|Tengu]]; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ([[:en:Personal grooming|Fur Care]]) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ([[:en:Hayashi (music)|Hayashi]]) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ([[:en:Drum|Taiko]]) and cymbals ([[:en:Kane (instrument)|Bell]]) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki [[Category:Kasuga Redirects]]s to 民俗文化財}} by Inabe City in May of [[:en:Shōwa era|Shōwa period]] 58 (1983).
