<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe)]][[ja:春日神社 (いなべ市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe)]][[ja:春日神社 (いなべ市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe)]][[ja:春日神社 (いなべ市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe)]][[ja:春日神社 (いなべ市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11513660]] Wikidata name: Kasuga-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11513660]] Wikidata name: Kasuga-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|Inabe|en|Inabe|WD=Q281817|lt=Inabe City}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) |location = {{ill|Inabe|WD=Q281817}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35.16286111111111|N|136.50147222222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}} {{ill|Inabe|en|Inabe|WD=Q281817|lt=Inabe City}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to オオヤマツミ|1=Ōyamatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q386563}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki s to カグツチ}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Kunado-no-Kami|en|Kunado-no-Kami|WD=Q2963653|lt=Kunado no Kami}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to イワナガヒメ|1=Iwanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191705}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 天児屋命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2564508}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|ja_comment=jawiki s to ミヅハノメ|1=Mizuhanome|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191796}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|ja_comment=jawiki s to 延喜式神名帳}} “{{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}[[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|1=Yoshitada Haruzumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513769}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 総社|1=sōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107129}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|WD=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei period}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|1=Tokugawa Institute for the History of Forestry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490286}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|ja_comment=jawiki s to 氏神|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ujigami|WD=Q767655}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Seokjeon|en|Seokjeon|WD=Q11585326|lt=Stone Battle}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei 22}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion dance|en|Lion dance|WD=Q713866|lt=Lion Dance}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese guardian lions|en|Chinese guardian lions|WD=Q1069772|lt=Lion}} and {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Personal grooming|en|Personal grooming|WD=Q999981|lt=Fur Care}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi (music)|en|Hayashi (music)|WD=Q4171186|lt=Hayashi}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Drum|en|Drum|WD=Q11404|lt=Taiko}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Kane (instrument)|en|Kane (instrument)|WD=Q2280552|lt=Bell}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki s to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== Footnote == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|en|Kadokawa Shoten|WD=Q1054765|lt=Kadokawa Shoten}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha}}, Shōwa 58
== External links == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm List of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Mie Prefecture] (Mie Prefectural Board of Education)

'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}} {{ill|Inabe|en|Inabe|WD=Q281817|lt=Inabe City}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to オオヤマツミ|1=Ōyamatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q386563}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki s to カグツチ}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Kunado-no-Kami|en|Kunado-no-Kami|WD=Q2963653|lt=Kunado no Kami}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to イワナガヒメ|1=Iwanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191705}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 天児屋命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2564508}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|ja_comment=jawiki s to ミヅハノメ|1=Mizuhanome|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191796}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|ja_comment=jawiki s to 延喜式神名帳}} “{{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}[[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|1=Yoshitada Haruzumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513769}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 総社|1=sōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107129}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|WD=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei period}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|1=Tokugawa Institute for the History of Forestry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490286}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|ja_comment=jawiki s to 氏神|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ujigami|WD=Q767655}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Seokjeon|en|Seokjeon|WD=Q11585326|lt=Stone Battle}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei 22}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion dance|en|Lion dance|WD=Q713866|lt=Lion Dance}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese guardian lions|en|Chinese guardian lions|WD=Q1069772|lt=Lion}} and {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Personal grooming|en|Personal grooming|WD=Q999981|lt=Fur Care}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi (music)|en|Hayashi (music)|WD=Q4171186|lt=Hayashi}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Drum|en|Drum|WD=Q11404|lt=Taiko}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Kane (instrument)|en|Kane (instrument)|WD=Q2280552|lt=Bell}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki s to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period}} 58 (1983).

{{Infobox religious building| |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|Inabe|en|Inabe|WD=Q281817|lt=Inabe City}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) |location = {{ill|Inabe|WD=Q281817}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35.16286111111111|N|136.50147222222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}} {{ill|Inabe|en|Inabe|WD=Q281817|lt=Inabe City}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to オオヤマツミ|1=Ōyamatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q386563}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki s to カグツチ}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Kunado-no-Kami|en|Kunado-no-Kami|WD=Q2963653|lt=Kunado no Kami}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to イワナガヒメ|1=Iwanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191705}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 天児屋命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2564508}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|ja_comment=jawiki s to ミヅハノメ|1=Mizuhanome|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191796}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|ja_comment=jawiki s to 延喜式神名帳}} “{{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}[[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|1=Yoshitada Haruzumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513769}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 総社|1=sōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107129}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|WD=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei period}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|1=Tokugawa Institute for the History of Forestry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490286}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|ja_comment=jawiki s to 氏神|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ujigami|WD=Q767655}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Seokjeon|en|Seokjeon|WD=Q11585326|lt=Stone Battle}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei 22}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion dance|en|Lion dance|WD=Q713866|lt=Lion Dance}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese guardian lions|en|Chinese guardian lions|WD=Q1069772|lt=Lion}} and {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Personal grooming|en|Personal grooming|WD=Q999981|lt=Fur Care}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi (music)|en|Hayashi (music)|WD=Q4171186|lt=Hayashi}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Drum|en|Drum|WD=Q11404|lt=Taiko}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Kane (instrument)|en|Kane (instrument)|WD=Q2280552|lt=Bell}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki s to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period}} 58 (1983).

Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|ja_comment=jawiki s to 延喜式神名帳}} “{{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}[[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|1=Yoshitada Haruzumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513769}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 総社|1=sōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107129}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|WD=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei period}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|1=Tokugawa Institute for the History of Forestry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490286}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|ja_comment=jawiki s to 氏神|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ujigami|WD=Q767655}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Seokjeon|en|Seokjeon|WD=Q11585326|lt=Stone Battle}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei 22}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion dance|en|Lion dance|WD=Q713866|lt=Lion Dance}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese guardian lions|en|Chinese guardian lions|WD=Q1069772|lt=Lion}} and {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Personal grooming|en|Personal grooming|WD=Q999981|lt=Fur Care}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi (music)|en|Hayashi (music)|WD=Q4171186|lt=Hayashi}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Drum|en|Drum|WD=Q11404|lt=Taiko}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Kane (instrument)|en|Kane (instrument)|WD=Q2280552|lt=Bell}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki s to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== Footnote == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|en|Kadokawa Shoten|WD=Q1054765|lt=Kadokawa Shoten}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha}}, Shōwa 58
== External links == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm List of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Mie Prefecture] (Mie Prefectural Board of Education)

{{Infobox religious building| |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|Inabe|en|Inabe|WD=Q281817|lt=Inabe City}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) |location = {{ill|Inabe|WD=Q281817}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35.16286111111111|N|136.50147222222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}} {{ill|Inabe|en|Inabe|WD=Q281817|lt=Inabe City}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to オオヤマツミ|1=Ōyamatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q386563}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki s to カグツチ}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Kunado-no-Kami|en|Kunado-no-Kami|WD=Q2963653|lt=Kunado no Kami}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to イワナガヒメ|1=Iwanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191705}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 天児屋命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2564508}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|ja_comment=jawiki s to ミヅハノメ|1=Mizuhanome|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191796}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|1=Yoshitada Haruzumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513769}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 総社|1=sōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107129}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|WD=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei period}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|1=Tokugawa Institute for the History of Forestry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490286}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.

According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|ja_comment=jawiki s to 氏神|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ujigami|WD=Q767655}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Seokjeon|en|Seokjeon|WD=Q11585326|lt=Stone Battle}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.

The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion dance|en|Lion dance|WD=Q713866|lt=Lion Dance}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese guardian lions|en|Chinese guardian lions|WD=Q1069772|lt=Lion}} and {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Personal grooming|en|Personal grooming|WD=Q999981|lt=Fur Care}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi (music)|en|Hayashi (music)|WD=Q4171186|lt=Hayashi}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Drum|en|Drum|WD=Q11404|lt=Taiko}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Kane (instrument)|en|Kane (instrument)|WD=Q2280552|lt=Bell}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki s to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period}} 58 (1983).

'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}} {{ill|Inabe|en|Inabe|WD=Q281817|lt=Inabe City}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to オオヤマツミ|1=Ōyamatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q386563}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki s to カグツチ}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Kunado-no-Kami|en|Kunado-no-Kami|WD=Q2963653|lt=Kunado no Kami}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to イワナガヒメ|1=Iwanaga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191705}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 天児屋命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2564508}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|ja_comment=jawiki s to ミヅハノメ|1=Mizuhanome|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191796}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|ja_comment=jawiki s to 延喜式神名帳}} “{{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province}}[[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|1=Yoshitada Haruzumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513769}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 総社|1=sōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107129}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|WD=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei period}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|1=Tokugawa Institute for the History of Forestry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490286}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|ja_comment=jawiki s to 氏神|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=ujigami|WD=Q767655}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Seokjeon|en|Seokjeon|WD=Q11585326|lt=Stone Battle}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei 22}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion dance|en|Lion dance|WD=Q713866|lt=Lion Dance}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese guardian lions|en|Chinese guardian lions|WD=Q1069772|lt=Lion}} and {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Personal grooming|en|Personal grooming|WD=Q999981|lt=Fur Care}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi (music)|en|Hayashi (music)|WD=Q4171186|lt=Hayashi}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Drum|en|Drum|WD=Q11404|lt=Taiko}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Kane (instrument)|en|Kane (instrument)|WD=Q2280552|lt=Bell}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki s to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property of Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance

{{Infobox religious building| |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|Inabe|en|Inabe|WD=Q281817|lt=Inabe City|1=Inabe}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) |location = {{ill|Inabe|WD=Q281817|1=Inabe}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35.16286111111111|N|136.50147222222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}} {{ill|Inabe|en|Inabe|WD=Q281817|lt=Inabe City|1=Inabe}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to オオヤマツミ|1=Ōyamatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q386563}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki s to カグツチ}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Kunado-no-Kami|en|Kunado-no-Kami|WD=Q2963653|lt=Kunado no Kami|1=Kunado-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to イワナガヒメ|1=Iwanaga-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191705}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 天児屋命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2564508}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|ja_comment=jawiki s to ミヅハノメ|1=Mizuhanome|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191796}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement|1=Bunrei}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|ja_comment=jawiki s to 延喜式神名帳}} “{{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province|1=Ise Province}}[[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|1=Yoshitada Haruzumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513769}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 総社|1=Sōja shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107129}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami|1=Ubusunagami}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|WD=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei period|1=Bunsei}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|1=Tokugawa Institute for the History of Forestry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490286}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.
== 祭祀 == Prior to the Meiji period, the festival day was August 14 (as recorded in the Inabe Miscellany). According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|ja_comment=jawiki s to 氏神|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Ujigami|WD=Q767655}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era|1=Ōei}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Seokjeon|en|Seokjeon|WD=Q11585326|lt=Stone Battle|1=Seokjeon}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei 22|1=Heisei era}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era|1=Kaei}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion dance|en|Lion dance|WD=Q713866|lt=Lion Dance|1=Lion dance}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese guardian lions|en|Chinese guardian lions|WD=Q1069772|lt=Lion|1=Chinese guardian lions}} and {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu|1=Tengu}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Personal grooming|en|Personal grooming|WD=Q999981|lt=Fur Care|1=Personal grooming}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi (music)|en|Hayashi (music)|WD=Q4171186|lt=Hayashi|1=Hayashi (music)}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Drum|en|Drum|WD=Q11404|lt=Taiko|1=Drum}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Kane (instrument)|en|Kane (instrument)|WD=Q2280552|lt=Bell|1=Kane (instrument)}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki s to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period|1=Shōwa era}} 58 (1983).
== Cultural Properties == * Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance
== Footnote == {{Footnotes Help}} {{reflist}}
== Bibliography == * 『三重県神社誌』, Mie Prefecture Shrine Office, Heisei 5 * 『Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Mie Prefecture』 (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Vol. 24), {{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|en|Kadokawa Shoten|WD=Q1054765|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|1=Kadokawa Shoten}}, Shōwa 58 * 『Place Names of Mie Prefecture』 (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei Vol. 24), {{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha|1=Heibonsha}}, Shōwa 58
== External links == *[http://www.pref.mie.jp/BUNKAZAI/HP/pages/A030/geinouichiran.htm List of Intangible Folk Cultural Properties of Mie Prefecture] (Mie Prefectural Board of Education)

{{Infobox religious building| |Name = Kasuga Shrine (春日神社) |image = File:Kasuga-jinja (Inabe), Torii.jpg |Location = {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}{{ill|Inabe|en|Inabe|WD=Q281817|lt=Inabe City|1=Inabe}}藤原町下野尻字炭焼951 |Coordinates = {{Coord|35|09|46.3|N|136|30|05.3|E|scale:25000}} |Enshrined Deities = {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}} and 10 others |Shrine Rank = Former Village Shrine |Founded = Unknown |Main Hall = |Alternate Name = |Fuda Site = |Annual Festival = October 8 |Rituals = Dedicated Lion Dance (October 9 & 10) |location = {{ill|Inabe|WD=Q281817|1=Inabe}} |coordinates = {{Coord|35.16286111111111|N|136.50147222222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}} {{ill|Inabe|en|Inabe|WD=Q281817|lt=Inabe City|1=Inabe}}. Its former shrine Rank is {{ill|Village Shrine|ja|村社|1=Son-sha|WD=Q134917284}}{{efn|Former shrine rank|lt=Son-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Village Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}}.
== 祭神 == {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, {{ill|Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja|大山祇神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōyamatsumi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to オオヤマツミ|1=Ōyamatsumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q386563}}, {{ill|Kagatsurami no Kami|ja|火産霊神|1=Kagu-tsuchi|lt=Kagutsuchi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagatsurami no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kagutsuchi|ja_comment=jawiki s to カグツチ}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja|八衢比古神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hiko no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja|八衢比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukuri-hime no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to 岐の神|1=Kunado-no-Kami|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2963653}}, {{ill|Kunado-no-Kami|en|Kunado-no-Kami|WD=Q2963653|lt=Kunado no Kami|1=Kunado-no-Kami}}, {{ill|Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja|磐長姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanaga-hime no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to イワナガヒメ|1=Iwanaga-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191705}}, {{ill|Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja|武甕槌神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-mikazuchi no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki s to タケミカヅチ|1=Takemikazuchi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1502690}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja|天児屋根命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 天児屋命|1=Ame-no-Koyane|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2564508}}, {{ill|Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja|経津主命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to 経津主神|1=Futsunushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107286}}, and {{ill|Moshō no Megami|ja|罔象女神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moshō no Megami|ja_comment=jawiki s to ミヅハノメ|1=Mizuhanome|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8191796}} are enshrined.
Among the enshrined deities, {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}} is said to be “the deity whose spirit resides in stone”<ref>From the Mie Prefecture Shrine Record dated August 10, 12th year of Ōei, titled “The Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident” (cited in 『三重県神社誌』).</ref>.
== 由緒 == The date and other details of {{ill|Bunrei|en|Bunrei|WD=Q195793|lt=Enshrinement|1=Bunrei}} are unknown. In ancient times, it was known as “Iharamiya”<ref>『角川日本地名大辞典』.</ref>. The Inabe District Local History Materials identifies this shrine as the “Stone Shrine” of the {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|ja_comment=jawiki s to 延喜式神名帳}} “{{ill|Ise Province|en|Ise Province|WD=Q864166|lt=Ise Province|1=Ise Province}}[[:en:Inabe District, Mie|Inabe District]] Stone Shrine,” enshrining {{ill|Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona|lt=Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja|少名彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sukunabikona no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki s to スクナビコナ}}, and records that a sacred stone emitting light every night existed on the grounds. This stone was revered by {{ill|Harusumi Zennawa|ja|春澄善縄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harusumi Zennawa|1=Yoshitada Haruzumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513769}}, who established the shrine and named it “Stone Shrine”<ref>Inabe District Office ed., 『員弁郡郷土資料』, Taishō 4 (1915).</ref>.
The 『伊勢輯雑記』 records that, as the principal shrine ({{ill|Main Shrine|ja|惣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Main Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki s to 総社|1=Sōja shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1107129}}) of the Shirose Seven Villages, it was once a grand shrine, while the Inabe Miscellany states that it is the tutelary deity ({{ill|Ubusunagami|en|Ubusunagami|WD=Q1392097|lt=Ubusunagami|1=Ubusunagami}}) of both Shimonojiri and Nishinojiri, remarking that “in ancient times, it was a magnificent shrine”<ref>『伊勢輯雑記』 by Matsumiya Yūsugu (from the people of Inabe District, born in Tenmei 7 (1787) – died in Kōka 2 (1845)). 『員弁雑誌』 was compiled by Okada Seidō in the early Meiji period as a corrected and supplemented section on Inabe District from 『伊勢輯雑記』.</ref>.
In the Village Detailed Account of the 7th year of {{ill|Bunsei|en|Bunsei|WD=Q1327119|lt=Bunsei period|1=Bunsei}} (1824), it is recorded as “Kasuga Daimyojin,” and in the Village Detailed Account of the 5th year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} (1872) it is referred to as “Ishihara Shrine”<ref>Both of these Detailed Accounts are preserved in the {{ill|Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|ja|徳川林政史研究所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Hayashi Historical Institute|1=Tokugawa Institute for the History of Forestry|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490286}}. The “Ishihara” seen in the Meiji account may be a misprint for “Ihara” (see 『三重県の地名』).</ref>. In Meiji 45, the small shrines dedicated to Hachitengū, Suijin, Yama-no-Kami, and the boundary deity were merged<ref>The Bunsei account lists “Atago, two Yama-no-Kami” as small village shrines, while the Meiji account lists “two Yama-no-Kami, Suijin, and Hashii no Kami.” Additionally, the Inabe Miscellany refers to Hachitengū as “Hachitengu-gu” and Atago as “without a shrine.”</ref>.

According to the Inabe District Local History Materials, during the festival the parishioners ({{ill|Parishioner|ja|氏子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Parishioner|ja_comment=jawiki s to 氏神|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Ujigami|WD=Q767655}}) would offer white stones, after which a “stone-throwing ritual” was performed; however, during the {{ill|Ōei|en|Ōei|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era|1=Ōei}} (late 14th to early 15th century), this was supplanted by the {{ill|Seokjeon|en|Seokjeon|WD=Q11585326|lt=Stone Battle|1=Seokjeon}} and subsequently prohibited<ref>As noted in the previous reference on the “Stone Shrine Stone Festival and Stone-throwing Incident”: although offering stones before the shrine can be understood as relating to the deity whose spirit dwells in stone, there is no traditional basis for stone throwing, and due to its dangerous nature, it was prohibited except for shrine officials.</ref>.
=== 獅子舞 === The Lion Dance is performed on both October 9 and 10, and as of {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei 22|1=Heisei era}} (2010) it is said to have a history of over 300 years. The lion head currently in use is a valuable artifact created in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Kaei|en|Kaei|WD=Q1146125|lt=Kaei era|1=Kaei}} (1849). The {{ill|Lion dance|en|Lion dance|WD=Q713866|lt=Lion Dance|1=Lion dance}} is enacted with roles by the {{ill|Chinese guardian lions|en|Chinese guardian lions|WD=Q1069772|lt=Lion|1=Chinese guardian lions}} and {{ill|Tengu|en|Tengu|WD=Q742360|lt=Tengu|1=Tengu}}; the lion performs two dances—“Dance of the Frenzied Peony” and “Dance of the Chrysanthemum,” which represent spring and autumn—while the tengu accompanies the lion, guiding and assisting the performance. The lion dance, incorporating natural animal behaviors such as grooming ({{ill|Personal grooming|en|Personal grooming|WD=Q999981|lt=Fur Care|1=Personal grooming}}) and sleeping while repelling calamities from all directions, is performed to pray for a bountiful harvest and the safety of the region. Additionally, festival music ({{ill|Hayashi (music)|en|Hayashi (music)|WD=Q4171186|lt=Hayashi|1=Hayashi (music)}}) is played, featuring children clad in kasuri-patterned kimonos with flower-crowned hats, who perform on instruments such as drums ({{ill|Drum|en|Drum|WD=Q11404|lt=Taiko|1=Drum}}) and cymbals ({{ill|Kane (instrument)|en|Kane (instrument)|WD=Q2280552|lt=Bell|1=Kane (instrument)}}) in time with an adult’s flute. The dance, known as the “Shimonojiri Kasuga Shrine Dedicated Lion Dance,” was designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki s to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}} by Inabe City in May of {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa period|1=Shōwa era}} 58 (1983).
